([/ˈbæstrəp, -trɒp/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a city and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Bastrop County, Texas](/wiki/Bastrop_County,_Texas "Bastrop County, Texas"), United States. The population was 9,688 according to the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[8]](#cite_note-0AuaI-8) It is located about 30 mi (48 km) southeast of [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") and is part of the [Greater Austin](/wiki/Greater_Austin "Greater Austin") metropolitan area.[[9]](#cite_note-wVLyL-9)

**Bastrop** ([/ˈbæstrəp, -trɒp/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a city and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Bastrop County, Texas](/wiki/Bastrop_County,_Texas "Bastrop County, Texas"), United States. The population was 9,688 according to the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[8]](#cite_note-0AuaI-8) It is located about 30 mi (48 km) southeast of [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") and is part of the [Greater Austin](/wiki/Greater_Austin "Greater Austin") metropolitan area.[[9]](#cite_note-wVLyL-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bastrop,_Texas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/BastropDowntown1.JPG/250px-BastropDowntown1.JPG)](/wiki/File:BastropDowntown1.JPG)

Downtown Bastrop

Spanish soldiers lived temporarily at the current site of Bastrop as early as 1804, when a fort was established where the [Old San Antonio Road](/wiki/Old_San_Antonio_Road "Old San Antonio Road") crossed the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)") and named *Puesta del Colorado*.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Bastrop's namesake, [Felipe Enrique Neri, Baron de Bastrop](/wiki/Felipe_Enrique_Neri,_Baron_de_Bastrop "Felipe Enrique Neri, Baron de Bastrop"), was a commoner named Philip Hendrik Nering Bogel, who was wanted for [embezzlement](/wiki/Embezzlement "Embezzlement") in his native country of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"). In Texas, he assisted [Moses](/wiki/Moses_Austin "Moses Austin") and [Stephen F. Austin](/wiki/Stephen_F._Austin "Stephen F. Austin") in obtaining land grants in Texas and served as Austin's land commissioner. In 1827, Austin located about 100 families in an area adjacent to his earlier Mexican contracts. Austin arranged for Mexican officials to name a new town there after the baron who died the same year.

On June 8, 1832, the town was platted along conventional Mexican lines, with a square in the center and blocks set aside for public buildings. The town was named Bastrop, but two years later, the [Coahuila y Tejas](/wiki/Coahuila_y_Tejas "Coahuila y Tejas") legislature renamed it Mina in honor of [Francisco Javier Mina](/wiki/Francisco_Javier_Mina "Francisco Javier Mina"), a Mexican revolutionary hero and [martyr](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr"). The town was incorporated under the laws of the [Republic of Texas](/wiki/Republic_of_Texas "Republic of Texas") on December 18, 1837, and the name was changed back to Bastrop.

Overlooking the center of the town is the [Lost Pines Forest](/wiki/Lost_Pines_Forest "Lost Pines Forest"). Composed of [loblolly pines](/wiki/Loblolly_pine "Loblolly pine") (*Pinus taeda*), the forest is the center of the westernmost stand of the [southern pine forest](/wiki/Piney_Woods "Piney Woods"). As the only timber available in the area, the forest contributed to the local economy. Bastrop began supplying Austin with lumber in 1839 and then [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"), the western Texas frontier, and parts of Mexico.

A fire in 1862 destroyed most of downtown Bastrop's commercial buildings and the county courthouse. As a result, most current downtown structures postdate the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). In 1979, the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") admitted 131 Bastrop buildings and sites to its listings. This earned Bastrop the title of the "Most Historic Small Town in Texas".

The first edition of the *Bastrop Advertiser and County News* (now *[The Bastrop Advertiser](/wiki/The_Bastrop_Advertiser "The Bastrop Advertiser")*) was published on March 1, 1853, giving it claim to be the oldest continuously published weekly (semiweekly since September 5, 1977) in Texas. The wider Bastrop County is also covered by papers such as the *[Elgin Courier](/wiki/Elgin_Courier "Elgin Courier")*.

On September 4, 2011, two wildfires started when trees fell on power lines. The first fire started in the community of [Circle D-KC Estates](/wiki/Circle_D-KC_Estates,_Texas "Circle D-KC Estates, Texas") near [Bastrop State Park](/wiki/Bastrop_State_Park "Bastrop State Park"), and the other fire started about 4 mi (6 km) north. The two fires merged into the [Bastrop County Complex fire](/wiki/Bastrop_County_Complex_fire "Bastrop County Complex fire"). This was the worst and most destructive wildfire in Texas history, as it destroyed 1,691 homes, killed two people, and caused $325 million of insured property damage. The drought in Texas at the time combined with strong winds from the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") caused by [Tropical Storm Lee](/wiki/Tropical_Storm_Lee_(2011) "Tropical Storm Lee (2011)") helped fuel the fire.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bastrop,_Texas&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Bastrop is located near the center of Bastrop County along the lower [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)"). The downtown business district of the city is located on a bluff on the east bank of the river, but the city extends to the west side of the river, as well. According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 9.1 square miles (23.6 km2), of which 9.0 sq mi (23.3 km2) are land and 0.12 square miles (0.3 km2), or 1.23%, is covered by water.[[11]](#cite_note-Census_2010-11)

Three miles (5 km) northeast of the town, [Lake Bastrop](/wiki/Lake_Bastrop "Lake Bastrop") is a 906-acre (3.67 km2) reservoir on Spicer Creek operated by the [Lower Colorado River Authority](/wiki/Lower_Colorado_River_Authority "Lower Colorado River Authority") (LCRA) since its impounding in 1964. Although primarily used as a cooling pond for the [Sim Gideon Power Plant](/wiki/Sim_Gideon_Power_Plant "Sim Gideon Power Plant"), the lake is also used for recreation, and the LCRA maintains two public parks on the lake.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bastrop,_Texas&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Climate")]

Bastrop tends to be cooler than other central Texas cities, but can reach 100 °F in the summer. Extreme temperatures range from –1 to 111 °F.

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bastrop,_Texas&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Bastrop had a population of 9,688 and 2,022 families residing in the city. The median age was 38.6 years, with 22.6% of residents under the age of 18 and 18.9% of residents 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 91.5 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 89.5 males age 18 and over.[[15]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-15)

96.4% of residents lived in urban areas, while 3.6% lived in rural areas.[[16]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-16)

There were 3,714 households in Bastrop, of which 32.4% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 43.4% were married-couple households, 16.8% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 33.2% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 30.0% of all households were made up of individuals and 13.5% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[15]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-15)

There were 4,089 housing units, of which 9.2% were vacant. Among occupied housing units, 52.2% were owner-occupied and 47.8% were renter-occupied. The homeowner vacancy rate was 3.1% and the rental vacancy rate was 7.6%.[[15]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-15)

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bastrop,_Texas&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[7]](#cite_note-GR2-7) of 2000, 5340 people resided in Bastrop, in 2034 households and 1336 families. The population density was 734.8 inhabitants per square mile (283.7/km2). The 2,239 housing units averaged 308.1 per square mile (118.9/km2). The [racial makeup](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)") of the city was 72.3% White, 17.0% African American, 1.0% Asian, 0.7% Native American, 7.0% from other races, and 1.9% from two or more races. About 17.8% of the population was Hispanic or Latino of any race.

Of the 2,034 households, 32.8% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 46.6% were married couples living together, 15.3% had a female householder with no husband present, and 34.3% were not families. About 29.4% of all households were made up of individuals, and 12.6% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.46 and the average family size was 3.05.

In the city, the population was distributed as 25.5% under the age of 18, 8.3% from 18 to 24, 29.6% from 25 to 44, 21.8% from 45 to 64, and 14.8% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 36 years. For every 100 females, there were 97.0 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 91.2 males.

The median income for a household in the city was $40,212, and for a family was $49,258. Males had a median income of $34,388 versus $27,582 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $19,862; 11.7% of the population and 10.1% of families were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"). Of the total population, 15.6% of those under the age of 18 and 13.6% of those 65 and older were living below the poverty line.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bastrop,_Texas&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy")]

As of 2020, the area's four largest employers are the Bastrop Independent [School District](/wiki/School_District "School District"), Hyatt Regency Lost Pines Resort and Spa, Bastrop County government, and MD Anderson Cancer Center.[[18]](#cite_note-2YKOr-18)

The [Hyatt](/wiki/Hyatt "Hyatt") Regency Lost Pines Resort and Spa (situated about 15 mi west of the City of Bastrop on 405 acres), opened on June 2, 2006, with 491 rooms and gave a boost to employment and sales tax in the area. When the property changed ownership in 2011, officials stated it employed 600 individuals plus 175 additional seasonal employees – making it the largest private employer in Bastrop County.[[19]](#cite_note-Bkh2h-19)

[Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk")'s companies have buildings located just outside Bastrop city limits, including [The Boring Company](/wiki/The_Boring_Company "The Boring Company") headquarters, a [SpaceX](/wiki/SpaceX "SpaceX") facility, and the headquarters of [X](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[Federal Correctional Institution Bastrop](/wiki/Federal_Correctional_Institution_Bastrop "Federal Correctional Institution Bastrop"), a prison of the [Federal Bureau of Prisons](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Prisons "Federal Bureau of Prisons"), is in nearby [Camp Swift](/wiki/Camp_Swift,_Texas "Camp Swift, Texas").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bastrop,_Texas&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Bastrop_tx_isd_hq.jpg/250px-Bastrop_tx_isd_hq.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bastrop_tx_isd_hq.jpg)

[Bastrop ISD](/wiki/Bastrop_ISD "Bastrop ISD") headquarters

The [Bastrop Independent School District](/wiki/Bastrop_Independent_School_District "Bastrop Independent School District") serves Bastrop. Students in grades K-5 attend either Adelton, Colony Oaks, Emile, or Mina Elementary. Students in grades 6-8 attend either Bastrop or Riverside Middle School. Students in grades 9-12 attend [Bastrop High School](/wiki/Bastrop_High_School_(Texas) "Bastrop High School (Texas)").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[Austin Community College](/wiki/Austin_Community_College "Austin Community College") conducts night and continuing-education classes at Bastrop High School.

From 1893 until 1969, [Emile High School](/wiki/Emile_High_School "Emile High School") served as the segregated black high school.[[23]](#cite_note-cMq9P-23)
